@@ -730,7 +730,7 @@ {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8w6y","title":"Implement Web Crypto compatible encryption for HTML export","description":"# Task: Web Crypto Compatible Encryption ## Context Enable optional password-based encryption for exported HTML files. The encryption must be performed in Rust, but decryption happens client-side using Web Crypto API. This requires careful alignment of cryptographic parameters. ## Architecture ### Server-Side (Rust) - src/html_export/encryption.rs 1. Generate cryptographic parameters 2. Derive key from password using Argon2id (Rust) -> export as PBKDF2 params for JS 3. Encrypt message payload with AES-256-GCM 4. Embed encrypted blob + decryption script in HTML ### Client-Side (JavaScript) 1. Prompt for password 2. Derive key using PBKDF2 (Web Crypto compatible) 3. Decrypt payload 4. Render conversation ## Key Decision: PBKDF2 for Interoperability While the project uses Argon2id internally, Web Crypto API doesn't support it natively. Options: - **PBKDF2**: Native Web Crypto support, well-tested, slightly weaker than Argon2id - **scrypt via library**: Requires loading external JS, increases complexity - **Argon2 WASM**: Complex, large binary size **Decision**: Use PBKDF2 for HTML export encryption (different from internal Argon2id). This is acceptable because: 1. The encrypted HTML is for sharing, not long-term storage 2. PBKDF2 with high iterations is still secure for this use case 3. Zero dependencies means smaller file size and better reliability ## Encryption Parameters ```rust struct HtmlEncryptionConfig { // PBKDF2 settings (Web Crypto compatible) pbkdf2_iterations: u32, // 600,000 (OWASP 2024 recommendation) salt_bytes: usize, // 16 bytes // AES-GCM settings aes_key_bits: usize, // 256 iv_bytes: usize, // 12 bytes (96 bits per GCM spec) tag_bytes: usize, // 16 bytes (128 bits) } impl Default for HtmlEncryptionConfig { fn default() -> Self { Self { pbkdf2_iterations: 600_000, salt_bytes: 16, aes_key_bits: 256, iv_bytes: 12, tag_bytes: 16, } } } ``` ## Rust Encryption Function ```rust pub fn encrypt_for_html( plaintext: &str, password: &str, ) -> Result { // 1. Generate random salt and IV let salt = generate_random_bytes(16); let iv = generate_random_bytes(12); // 2. Derive key using PBKDF2-SHA256 let key = pbkdf2_sha256(password.as_bytes(), &salt, 600_000, 32); // 3. Encrypt with AES-256-GCM let (ciphertext, tag) = aes_gcm_encrypt(&key, &iv, plaintext.as_bytes(), &[])?; // 4. Return payload with all params for JS decryption Ok(HtmlEncryptedPayload { version: 1, salt: base64_encode(&salt), iv: base64_encode(&iv), ciphertext: base64_encode(&ciphertext), tag: base64_encode(&tag), iterations: 600_000, }) } ``` ## JavaScript Decryption ```javascript async function decryptPayload(password) { const payload = JSON.parse(document.getElementById('encrypted-data').textContent); // 1. Decode base64 params const salt = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.salt); const iv = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.iv); const ciphertext = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.ciphertext); const tag = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.tag); // 2. Import password as key material const keyMaterial = await crypto.subtle.importKey( 'raw', new TextEncoder().encode(password), 'PBKDF2', false, ['deriveBits', 'deriveKey'] ); // 3. Derive AES key using PBKDF2 const key = await crypto.subtle.deriveKey( { name: 'PBKDF2', salt: salt, iterations: payload.iterations, hash: 'SHA-256' }, keyMaterial, { name: 'AES-GCM', length: 256 }, false, ['decrypt'] ); // 4. Decrypt (Web Crypto expects ciphertext+tag concatenated) const combined = new Uint8Array(ciphertext.byteLength + tag.byteLength); combined.set(new Uint8Array(ciphertext), 0); combined.set(new Uint8Array(tag), ciphertext.byteLength); const decrypted = await crypto.subtle.decrypt( { name: 'AES-GCM', iv: iv }, key, combined ); return new TextDecoder().decode(decrypted); } ``` ## Password Modal UI ```html

🔐 Password Required

This conversation is encrypted. Enter the password to view.

``` ## Security Considerations 1. **No password validation**: Wrong password = decryption failure (not explicit error) 2. **Timing attacks**: Use constant-time comparison where possible 3. **Memory clearing**: Clear password from memory after key derivation (JS limitation) 4. **XSS prevention**: Decrypted content sanitized before rendering ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Encryption works with all printable ASCII + Unicode passwords - [ ] Decryption works in Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge - [ ] Wrong password shows user-friendly error - [ ] Correct password reveals full conversation - [ ] No password hints or validation exposed - [ ] Cross-platform test: encrypt on Linux, decrypt on macOS","notes":"### Testing & Logging - Unit: encrypt/decrypt roundtrip with fixed vectors; validate PBKDF2 params. - Integration: browser decrypt test using headless webkit/chromium; log timing. - E2E: full export -> open -> decrypt script emits JSON report + failure screenshots.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T20:28:25.888891Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T23:05:20.354044Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T23:05:20.353962Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["html-export"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-8w6y","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-w9z0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:53Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-91vfy","title":"Wire background dispatch for state load/save and index refresh in TUI","notes":"Root cause validated: StateLoad/StateSave/IndexRefresh messages are wired but no-op in src/ui/app.rs; implementing background task dispatch + completion/failure handling and CLI reset-state wiring.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T20:55:14.088194Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T21:38:52.846540Z","closed_at":"2026-02-07T21:38:52.846505Z","close_reason":"Implemented background state load/save + index refresh dispatch in CassApp; wired --reset-state in CLI; added regression tests; validated with check/clippy and targeted tests.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-9210b91f","title":"Dry-Run Mode (--dry-run)","description":"# Dry-Run Mode (--dry-run) ## Problem Statement Agents want to validate queries without executing them: - Is the syntax valid? - How will it be interpreted? - What would the cost be? Currently, the only way to check is to run the query and examine results. ## Proposed Solution Add `--dry-run` flag that validates and analyzes without executing: ```bash cass search "complex query" --dry-run --json ``` Output: ```json { "dry_run": true, "valid": true, "explanation": {...}, "estimated_results": "10-100", "estimated_cost_ms": "<50", "warnings": [] } ``` ## Design Decisions ### What Dry-Run Does 1. Parse the query (full validation) 2. Analyze filters and constraints 3. Estimate result count (if possible) 4. Estimate execution time 5. Return without actually searching ### What Dry-Run Does NOT Do - Access the search index - Return actual results - Modify any state ### Estimation Accuracy Result count estimation could use: - Index statistics (total documents, term frequencies) - Historical query patterns - Simple heuristics Don't over-engineer; rough estimates are valuable. ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] `--dry-run` returns without executing search - [ ] Invalid queries return `valid: false` with error details - [ ] Valid queries include explanation - [ ] Response time is <10ms (no index access) - [ ] Works with all query types ## Effort Estimate Low-Medium - 2-3 hours. Query parsing is already done; this skips execution.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-30T23:54:08.438234Z","updated_at":"2025-12-15T06:23:15.005415Z","closed_at":"2025-12-02T05:06:06.678765Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} -{"id":"coding_agent_session_search-92o31","title":"[MEDIUM] mock-finder: pages encrypt sync_tree does not sync parent directory after subtree fsync","description":"Mock-code-finder finding: partial-sync-surface mock. ## Location \`src/pages/encrypt.rs:390-394\`: \`\`\`rust #[cfg(not(windows))] fn sync_tree(path: &Path) -> Result<()> { sync_tree_inner(path)?; Ok(()) } \`\`\` Compare to the sibling implementation in \`src/pages/bundle.rs:457-461\`: \`\`\`rust #[cfg(not(windows))] fn sync_tree(path: &Path) -> Result<()> { sync_tree_inner(path)?; sync_parent_directory(path) // ← explicit parent fsync } \`\`\` Both are named \`sync_tree\`, both claim to durably flush a tree, both are on the non-Windows path. But \`encrypt.rs\`'s version does NOT fsync the PARENT directory after the subtree completes. The \`sync_tree_inner\` routine fsyncs every file and directory WITHIN \`path\`, but the parent's directory-entry for \`path\` itself is not flushed. On power-loss, the FS journal can lose the rename/create that brought \`path\` into existence — the subtree is durable but the pointer to it is not. ## Impact \`src/pages/encrypt.rs\` is the encrypted-HTML-export flush path. If \`cass pages --encrypt\` succeeds, returns, then the machine loses power BEFORE the parent-directory fsync propagates, the encrypted archive's enclosing directory may not list it on remount. The files exist in the FS blocks but cannot be reached by path. The operator sees a missing export and a "success" exit code. \`bundle.rs\` has the correct two-step: subtree fsync + parent fsync. \`encrypt.rs\` only does the first step. ## Minimal repro Code review: diff the two functions. \`bundle.rs:460\` calls \`sync_parent_directory(path)\`, \`encrypt.rs:393\` does \`Ok(())\` instead. A crash-injection test (drop power between sync_tree and return) would be the reproducer, but this is a durability gap inferred from code comparison rather than observed in production (yet). ## Suggested fix Mirror \`bundle.rs\`: \`\`\`rust #[cfg(not(windows))] fn sync_tree(path: &Path) -> Result<()> { sync_tree_inner(path)?; sync_parent_directory(path) } \`\`\` \`src/pages/encrypt.rs\` already has a \`sync_parent_directory\` helper adjacent (src/pages/encrypt.rs contains a parallel cfg(not(windows)) parent-sync fn). Just add the call. Add a regression test in tests/pages_encrypt.rs: - Verify that after an encrypted export, the EXPECTED parent directory reports its mtime updated past the moment the export started (a proxy for "parent was fsynced"). - Or: assert behavior-wise that the export file appears under \`fs::read_dir(parent)\` immediately after the function returns (trivially true on warm FS, but documents the contract). ## Severity MEDIUM. Durability gap affects encrypted HTML archives — arguably higher-value artifacts than plain ones, since users specifically asked for encryption. Probability is low (requires power-loss timing), but the fix is a one-line addition to match the proven sibling impl. Labels: pages, encryption, crash-safety, durability, mock-finder.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:34:05.061372893Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:34:05.061372893Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} +{"id":"coding_agent_session_search-92o31","title":"[MEDIUM] mock-finder: pages encrypt sync_tree does not sync parent directory after subtree fsync","description":"Mock-code-finder finding: partial-sync-surface mock. ## Location \`src/pages/encrypt.rs:390-394\`: \`\`\`rust #[cfg(not(windows))] fn sync_tree(path: &Path) -> Result<()> { sync_tree_inner(path)?; Ok(()) } \`\`\` Compare to the sibling implementation in \`src/pages/bundle.rs:457-461\`: \`\`\`rust #[cfg(not(windows))] fn sync_tree(path: &Path) -> Result<()> { sync_tree_inner(path)?; sync_parent_directory(path) // ← explicit parent fsync } \`\`\` Both are named \`sync_tree\`, both claim to durably flush a tree, both are on the non-Windows path. But \`encrypt.rs\`'s version does NOT fsync the PARENT directory after the subtree completes. The \`sync_tree_inner\` routine fsyncs every file and directory WITHIN \`path\`, but the parent's directory-entry for \`path\` itself is not flushed. On power-loss, the FS journal can lose the rename/create that brought \`path\` into existence — the subtree is durable but the pointer to it is not. ## Impact \`src/pages/encrypt.rs\` is the encrypted-HTML-export flush path. If \`cass pages --encrypt\` succeeds, returns, then the machine loses power BEFORE the parent-directory fsync propagates, the encrypted archive's enclosing directory may not list it on remount. The files exist in the FS blocks but cannot be reached by path. The operator sees a missing export and a "success" exit code. \`bundle.rs\` has the correct two-step: subtree fsync + parent fsync. \`encrypt.rs\` only does the first step. ## Minimal repro Code review: diff the two functions. \`bundle.rs:460\` calls \`sync_parent_directory(path)\`, \`encrypt.rs:393\` does \`Ok(())\` instead. A crash-injection test (drop power between sync_tree and return) would be the reproducer, but this is a durability gap inferred from code comparison rather than observed in production (yet). ## Suggested fix Mirror \`bundle.rs\`: \`\`\`rust #[cfg(not(windows))] fn sync_tree(path: &Path) -> Result<()> { sync_tree_inner(path)?; sync_parent_directory(path) } \`\`\` \`src/pages/encrypt.rs\` already has a \`sync_parent_directory\` helper adjacent (src/pages/encrypt.rs contains a parallel cfg(not(windows)) parent-sync fn). Just add the call. Add a regression test in tests/pages_encrypt.rs: - Verify that after an encrypted export, the EXPECTED parent directory reports its mtime updated past the moment the export started (a proxy for "parent was fsynced"). - Or: assert behavior-wise that the export file appears under \`fs::read_dir(parent)\` immediately after the function returns (trivially true on warm FS, but documents the contract). ## Severity MEDIUM. Durability gap affects encrypted HTML archives — arguably higher-value artifacts than plain ones, since users specifically asked for encryption. Probability is low (requires power-loss timing), but the fix is a one-line addition to match the proven sibling impl. Labels: pages, encryption, crash-safety, durability, mock-finder.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:34:05.061372893Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:43:12.568353152Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-94f61ee9","title":"Status Command (cass status)","description":"# Status Command (cass status) ## Problem Statement Agents need a quick way to check system health before running searches: - Is the index fresh? - How many sessions are indexed? - Are there pending updates? Currently requires running `cass diag` which is verbose and slow. ## Proposed Solution Add lightweight `cass status` command: ```bash cass status --json ``` Output: ```json { "healthy": true, "index": { "fresh": false, "last_indexed_at": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z", "age_seconds": 3600, "stale": true, "stale_threshold_seconds": 1800 }, "database": { "conversations": 150, "messages": 4500 }, "pending": { "new_sessions": 5, "modified_sessions": 2, "connectors_with_updates": ["claude", "codex"] }, "cache": { "entries": 47, "hit_rate": 0.85 }, "recommended_action": "run 'cass index' to update 7 sessions" } ``` ## Design Decisions ### Staleness Definition Index is "stale" if: - `last_indexed_at` is older than threshold (default 30 minutes) - There are pending sessions detected via watch_state ### Performance Status should be FAST (<100ms): - Read watch_state.json for pending detection - Read meta table for last_indexed_at - Read cache stats from memory - NO index scanning ### Recommended Action Include human/agent-readable suggestion based on state. ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] `cass status --json` returns health summary - [ ] Response time <100ms - [ ] `fresh` accurately reflects index state - [ ] `pending` shows sessions awaiting indexing - [ ] `recommended_action` provides actionable guidance - [ ] Works without --json for human-readable output ## Effort Estimate Medium - 3-4 hours. Requires aggregating state from multiple sources.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-30T23:54:08.438234Z","updated_at":"2025-12-01T02:06:28.469087Z","closed_at":"2025-12-01T02:06:28.469087Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-94pe","title":"Model management system (download, verify, state)","description":"## Purpose Complete model management: manifest, state machine, download, verification, upgrades. ## Model Manifest (models.manifest.toml) ```toml [[models]] id = "all-minilm-l6-v2" repo = "sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2" revision = "e4ce9877abf3edfe10b0d82785e83bdcb973e22e" # Pinned! files = [ { name = "model.onnx", sha256 = "...", size = 22713856 }, { name = "tokenizer.json", sha256 = "...", size = 711396 }, { name = "config.json", sha256 = "...", size = 612 }, ] license = "Apache-2.0" # For future version updates [models.upgrade] check_url = "https://api.github.com/repos/.../releases/latest" # Optional notify_only = true # Don't auto-upgrade, just notify ``` ## State Machine ```rust pub enum ModelState { NotInstalled, NeedsConsent, Downloading { progress_pct: u8, bytes: u64, total: u64 }, Verifying, Ready, Disabled { reason: String }, VerificationFailed { reason: String }, UpdateAvailable { current: String, latest: String }, // ← ADDED } ``` ## Model Version Upgrades When we ship a new model version (e.g., better model, security fix): 1. Update models.manifest.toml in new cass release 2. On startup, compare installed model revision vs manifest 3. If mismatch: set state to UpdateAvailable (notify only, don't auto-upgrade) 4. User runs `cass models install --upgrade` to update 5. After model update, vector index is automatically rebuilt Why notify-only? Auto-upgrading could: - Use bandwidth unexpectedly - Break workflows if new model has different behavior - Cause confusion if results change ## Download System - Resumable (HTTP Range header) - Progress reporting via channel - Exponential backoff (3 retries, 5s → 15s → 45s) - Timeout: 5 minutes per file - Clean up partial files on cancel/failure ## Verification + Atomic Install - Download to models/.downloading/ - Verify SHA256 for each file - Atomic rename to models// - Write .verified marker with timestamp + revision ## Index Migration on Model Change When model revision changes: 1. Detect: installed model revision != index metadata embedder_revision 2. Mark semantic as unavailable temporarily 3. Rebuild vector index in background 4. Notify user: "Rebuilding semantic index for new model..." 5. When complete, semantic becomes available ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Full download → verify → install flow - [ ] Partial download resumes correctly - [ ] Corrupt download detected and retried - [ ] State transitions are correct - [ ] No network without explicit consent - [ ] Model version mismatch detected on startup - [ ] Index rebuild triggered after model upgrade - [ ] Cancel download cleans up partial files ## Depends On - sem.emb.ml (FastEmbed embedder) ## References - Plan: Section 3 (Network Policy, Consent, Model Management)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"OpusAgent","created_at":"2025-12-19T01:24:44.625634Z","updated_at":"2026-01-05T22:59:36.432582Z","closed_at":"2026-01-05T16:00:19.325730Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-94pe","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-mwsa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:54Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-96bi","title":"Document no-mock policy + coverage workflow","description":"Update TESTING.md/README.md with no-mock policy, fixture guidance, and coverage generation steps.\n\nDetails:\n- Explain model fixtures, SSH fixture host, PTY TUI tests.\n- Document log artifacts and how to inspect them.","acceptance_criteria":"1) TESTING.md documents no-mock policy, fixtures, and E2E logging layout. 2) README references testing commands and artifact locations. 3) Instructions include CI-only Playwright guidance.","notes":"Notes: - Keep docs concise but operationally complete. - Include examples for locating logs by trace_id.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T18:16:28.453962Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:16:11.936165Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T23:16:11.936034Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-96bi","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-1o9u","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:54Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-96bi","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-2mmt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:54Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-96bi","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-2r76","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:54Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-96bi","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-3jv0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:54Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]} @@ -1332,7 +1332,7 @@ {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-x399","title":"Task 1: Design MessageGroup data structure for consolidated rendering","description":"# Objective Create new data structures that represent GROUPED messages - a primary message plus all its associated tool calls and results. ## Current Problem In lib.rs:10254-10302, each message becomes an individual Message struct. There's no concept of grouping. ## Design ### New Types (in renderer.rs) ```rust /// Type of message group for rendering decisions #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)] pub enum MessageGroupType { User, Assistant, System, ToolOnly, // Orphan tool calls without parent message } /// A group of related messages for consolidated rendering. #[derive(Debug, Clone)] pub struct MessageGroup { /// Group type for rendering decisions pub group_type: MessageGroupType, /// The primary message (user or assistant text) pub primary: Message, /// Tool calls paired with their results pub tool_calls: Vec, /// Timestamp range for the entire interaction pub start_timestamp: Option, pub end_timestamp: Option, } /// Tool call paired with its result for correlation #[derive(Debug, Clone)] pub struct ToolCallWithResult { /// The original tool call pub call: ToolCall, /// The result (if received) pub result: Option, /// Correlation ID (tool_use_id in Claude format) pub correlation_id: Option, } /// Extended tool result with status and content #[derive(Debug, Clone)] pub struct ToolResult { /// Tool name this responds to pub tool_name: String, /// Result content (may be truncated for display) pub content: String, /// Execution status pub status: ToolStatus, /// Correlation ID to match with call pub correlation_id: Option, } impl MessageGroup { pub fn new(primary: Message, group_type: MessageGroupType) -> Self; pub fn add_tool_call(&mut self, call: ToolCall, correlation_id: Option); pub fn add_tool_result(&mut self, result: ToolResult); pub fn tool_count(&self) -> usize; pub fn has_errors(&self) -> bool; } ``` ### Key Design Decisions 1. **Correlation by ID**: Claude uses tool_use_id to link calls/results 2. **Paired storage**: ToolCallWithResult keeps call+result together 3. **Timestamp range**: Group tracks start/end times 4. **Group type enum**: Different rendering for user/assistant/system ## Files to Modify - src/html_export/renderer.rs ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] MessageGroup struct with all fields - [ ] MessageGroupType enum - [ ] ToolCallWithResult for paired storage - [ ] ToolResult struct - [ ] impl blocks with helper methods - [ ] Proper derives (Debug, Clone) - [ ] Doc comments - [ ] No compiler errors - [ ] Tracing logs for group operations","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:55:45.053453Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T22:40:46.344635Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T22:40:46.344524Z","close_reason":"Implemented MessageGroup, MessageGroupType, ToolCallWithResult, and ToolResult types with full impl blocks","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-x399","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-2jxn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-x4sj","title":"Implement remote index triggering","description":"# Implement remote index triggering ## What After installing cass on a remote machine (or if it was already installed but never indexed), trigger the initial indexing process so that session data is ready to sync. ## Why Syncing works by pulling from the remote's indexed data. If the remote has never run `cass index`, there's nothing meaningful to sync. The setup wizard should ensure remotes are indexed before attempting sync. This also provides value to users who may not realize they need to index on each machine. ## Technical Design ### Index Status Detection The probe phase already detects index status via `cass health --json`. This tells us: - Whether index exists - Session count - Last index timestamp - Index health ### Skip Logic Skip indexing if: - cass health reports healthy index with sessions - Index timestamp is recent (< 24 hours) - User explicitly passed --skip-index ### RemoteIndexer Implementation ```rust pub struct RemoteIndexer { host: String, ssh_timeout: u64, } pub struct IndexProgress { pub stage: IndexStage, pub message: String, pub sessions_found: u64, pub sessions_indexed: u64, } pub enum IndexStage { Starting, Scanning { agent: String }, Building, Complete, Failed { error: String }, } pub struct IndexResult { pub success: bool, pub sessions_indexed: u64, pub duration: Duration, pub error: Option, } impl RemoteIndexer { /// Check if indexing is needed based on probe result pub fn needs_indexing(probe: &HostProbeResult) -> bool { match &probe.cass_status { CassStatus::NotFound => true, // Just installed, needs index CassStatus::InstalledNotIndexed { .. } => true, CassStatus::Indexed { session_count, .. } => *session_count == 0, CassStatus::Unknown => true, } } /// Run indexing on remote host pub async fn run_index( &self, on_progress: impl Fn(IndexProgress), ) -> Result { let start = Instant::now(); on_progress(IndexProgress { stage: IndexStage::Starting, message: "Starting index...".into(), sessions_found: 0, sessions_indexed: 0, }); // Run cass index with streaming output let result = self.run_ssh_command_streaming( "cass index --progress", |line| { if let Some(progress) = parse_index_progress(&line) { on_progress(progress); } } ).await?; // Get final count let health = self.run_ssh_command("cass health --json").await?; let session_count = parse_session_count(&health); Ok(IndexResult { success: result.success, sessions_indexed: session_count, duration: start.elapsed(), error: result.error, }) } } ``` ### Long-Running Index Handling For hosts with many sessions (100k+), indexing can take 10+ minutes: ```rust /// For long indexes, use background execution with polling async fn run_long_index(&self, on_progress: impl Fn(IndexProgress)) -> Result { // Start index in background self.run_ssh_command("nohup cass index > ~/.cass_index.log 2>&1 &").await?; // Poll progress loop { let log = self.run_ssh_command("tail -20 ~/.cass_index.log").await?; if let Some(progress) = parse_index_progress(&log) { on_progress(progress); if progress.stage == IndexStage::Complete { break; } } tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(2)).await; } // Get final result self.get_index_result().await } ``` ### Progress Display ``` Indexing sessions on yto... Scanning ~/.claude/projects... found 234 sessions Scanning ~/.codex/sessions... found 12 sessions Scanning ~/.gemini/tmp... found 45 sessions Building search index... ████████████████████████████████████░░░░░░ 75% (219/291) ✓ Indexed 291 sessions on yto (45s) ``` ### Error Handling ```rust pub enum IndexError { SshFailed(String), CassNotFound, IndexFailed { stdout: String, stderr: String, exit_code: i32, }, DiskFull, Timeout, } fn handle_index_error(error: &IndexError) -> String { match error { IndexError::DiskFull => { "Disk full on remote. Free space and retry." } IndexError::Timeout => { "Index timed out. Try running manually: ssh host 'cass index'" } IndexError::IndexFailed { stderr, .. } if stderr.contains("permission denied") => { "Permission error. Check file permissions in agent data directories." } _ => "Index failed. See error details above." } } ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Detects whether indexing is needed from probe results - [ ] Skips indexing if already indexed with sessions - [ ] Triggers `cass index` on remote via SSH - [ ] Streams indexing progress to terminal - [ ] Reports session count after indexing - [ ] Handles long-running indexes (10+ min) without timeout - [ ] Handles failures gracefully with helpful messages - [ ] Works with freshly-installed cass ## Dependencies - Requires: Remote installation (coding_agent_session_search-o6ax) - if cass wasn't installed - Requires: SSH probing (coding_agent_session_search-vxe2) - to know if indexing needed ## Edge Cases - Very large session history (100k+ sessions) → use background + polling - Disk fills up during index → detect and report - Index partially completes → should be resumable via `cass index` - User cancels during index → remote index continues, can check later","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-05T13:08:15.203886Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-01-05T16:19:50.653795Z","closed_at":"2026-01-05T16:19:50.653795Z","close_reason":"Implemented RemoteIndexer in src/sources/index.rs with needs_indexing(), run_index(), progress streaming, and nohup+polling for long-running indexes (commit f083d68)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["indexing","sources","ssh"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-x4sj","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-o6ax","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-x4sj","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-vxe2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-x4xb","title":"P5.3: Safety Confirmations","description":"# P5.3: Safety Confirmations ## Goal Implement a multi-step confirmation flow that ensures users explicitly acknowledge the implications of publishing encrypted content to a public GitHub Pages site, preventing accidental or uninformed publishing. ## Background & Rationale ### Why Confirmation Gates Matter Publishing to GitHub Pages is: 1. **Public**: Anyone with the URL can access the encrypted archive 2. **Persistent**: GitHub retains history; deletion is not immediate 3. **Indexable**: Search engines may discover and cache the URL 4. **Irreversible**: Once published, copies may exist elsewhere ### Behavioral Safeguards Users should not be able to "click through" without reading. The confirmation flow should: 1. Require deliberate action (not just pressing Enter) 2. Display specific warnings tailored to the users configuration 3. Provide clear abort options at every stage 4. Confirm understanding, not just acceptance ## Technical Implementation ### Confirmation Steps ```rust pub enum ConfirmationStep { SecretScanAcknowledgment, ContentReview, PublicPublishingWarning, PasswordStrengthConfirmation, RecoveryKeyBackup, FinalConfirmation, } pub struct ConfirmationFlow { current_step: ConfirmationStep, completed_steps: HashSet, export_config: ExportConfig, summary: PrePublishSummary, } impl ConfirmationFlow { pub fn next_step(&mut self) -> Option { match self.current_step { ConfirmationStep::SecretScanAcknowledgment => { if self.summary.secret_scan.has_findings() { Some(ConfirmationStep::SecretScanAcknowledgment) } else { // Skip if no secrets found self.advance_to(ConfirmationStep::ContentReview) } } // ... handle other transitions } } pub fn can_proceed(&self) -> bool { self.completed_steps.contains(&ConfirmationStep::FinalConfirmation) } } ``` ### Step 1: Secret Scan Acknowledgment Only shown if secrets were detected: ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ ⚠️ SECRETS DETECTED │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ The secret scan found potential sensitive data: │ │ │ │ • 2 CRITICAL findings (private keys) │ │ • 3 HIGH findings (API keys) │ │ • 8 MEDIUM findings (potential passwords) │ │ │ │ Even though the export will be encrypted, publishing │ │ content containing secrets carries additional risk: │ │ │ │ ⚠️ If your password is weak or shared, secrets could be │ │ exposed through brute-force attacks. │ │ │ │ ⚠️ Secrets may remain valid and could be misused if │ │ encryption is ever compromised. │ │ │ │ RECOMMENDED: Remove or rotate any detected secrets before │ │ proceeding. │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Type "I understand the risks" to proceed: │ │ > _ │ │ │ │ [V] View findings [E] Exclude content [A] Abort │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` The user must type the exact phrase to proceed. ### Step 2: Content Review ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 📋 CONTENT REVIEW │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ You are about to export: │ │ │ │ • 156 conversations from 12 workspaces │ │ • 2,847 messages spanning 205 days │ │ • Content from: Claude Code, Aider, Codex │ │ │ │ This includes discussions about: │ │ • Code implementation details │ │ • Bug fixes and debugging sessions │ │ • Architecture decisions │ │ • Configuration and setup │ │ │ │ Have you reviewed the content summary? │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Press [Y] to confirm you have reviewed the content │ │ Press [R] to return to the summary │ │ Press [A] to abort │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Step 3: Public Publishing Warning ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 🌐 PUBLIC PUBLISHING WARNING │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ You are about to publish to: │ │ │ │ https://yourusername.github.io/cass-export/ │ │ │ │ IMPORTANT: │ │ │ │ ⚠️ This URL will be publicly accessible on the internet │ │ │ │ ⚠️ Anyone with the URL can download the encrypted archive │ │ │ │ ⚠️ GitHub retains history - deletion is not instantaneous │ │ │ │ ⚠️ Search engines may index this URL over time │ │ │ │ ⚠️ The security of your data depends entirely on the │ │ strength of your password and keeping it secret │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Type the following to confirm you understand: │ │ │ │ "publish to yourusername.github.io" │ │ > _ │ │ │ │ [A] Abort │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` User must type the exact target domain. ### Step 4: Password Strength Confirmation Only shown if password entropy is below threshold: ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 🔐 PASSWORD STRENGTH WARNING │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Your password has estimated entropy of 42 bits. │ │ │ │ Recommended minimum: 80 bits │ │ │ │ A password with 42 bits of entropy could potentially be │ │ cracked by a determined attacker with sufficient resources. │ │ │ │ For long-term security, consider: │ │ • Using a longer password (16+ characters) │ │ • Including numbers, symbols, and mixed case │ │ • Using a passphrase of 5+ random words │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ [S] Set stronger password │ │ [P] Proceed with current password (not recommended) │ │ [A] Abort │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Step 5: Recovery Key Backup ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 💾 BACKUP YOUR RECOVERY KEY │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Your recovery key has been generated. This is the ONLY way │ │ to recover your data if you forget your password. │ │ │ │ Recovery Key: │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐│ │ │ forge-table-river-cloud-dance-north-seven-quiet-blade ││ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘│ │ │ │ Store this key in a safe place: │ │ • Password manager │ │ • Printed and stored securely │ │ • Encrypted note │ │ │ │ ⚠️ If you lose both your password AND this recovery key, │ │ your data will be permanently inaccessible. │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Confirm you have saved the recovery key: │ │ │ │ Type the LAST word of the recovery key: _ │ │ │ │ [C] Copy to clipboard [A] Abort │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` User must type the last word to prove they read it. ### Step 6: Final Confirmation ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ ✓ FINAL CONFIRMATION │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ Ready to publish: │ │ │ │ ✓ Content reviewed (156 conversations) │ │ ✓ Secrets acknowledged (3 findings accepted) │ │ ✓ Public URL confirmed │ │ ✓ Password strength: STRONG (87 bits) │ │ ✓ Recovery key saved │ │ │ │ Target: https://yourusername.github.io/cass-export/ │ │ Size: ~450 KB │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Press ENTER twice to publish, or [A] to abort │ │ │ │ [ENTER] ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ [ENTER] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` Require two distinct keypresses to prevent accidental triggering. ### Implementation ```rust pub struct ConfirmationUI { flow: ConfirmationFlow, input_buffer: String, error_message: Option, } impl ConfirmationUI { pub fn handle_input(&mut self, key: KeyEvent) -> ConfirmationResult { match key.code { KeyCode::Char(c) => { self.input_buffer.push(c); ConfirmationResult::Continue } KeyCode::Enter => { if self.validate_current_step() { self.flow.complete_current_step(); if self.flow.can_proceed() { ConfirmationResult::Confirmed } else { self.flow.advance(); ConfirmationResult::Continue } } else { self.error_message = Some(self.get_validation_error()); ConfirmationResult::Continue } } KeyCode::Char('a') if key.modifiers.is_empty() => { ConfirmationResult::Aborted } _ => ConfirmationResult::Continue } } fn validate_current_step(&self) -> bool { match self.flow.current_step { ConfirmationStep::SecretScanAcknowledgment => { self.input_buffer.to_lowercase() == "i understand the risks" } ConfirmationStep::PublicPublishingWarning => { let expected = format!("publish to {}", self.flow.export_config.target_domain); self.input_buffer.to_lowercase() == expected.to_lowercase() } ConfirmationStep::RecoveryKeyBackup => { let last_word = self.flow.recovery_key.split('-').last().unwrap_or(""); self.input_buffer.to_lowercase() == last_word.to_lowercase() } // ... other validations } } } ``` ### Abort at Any Stage Every screen must have a clear abort option that: 1. Confirms the user wants to abort 2. Explains what happens (nothing published, local files cleaned up) 3. Returns to main menu ```rust fn handle_abort(&mut self) -> ConfirmationResult { // Show confirmation let confirmed = self.show_abort_confirmation(); if confirmed { // Clean up any temporary files self.cleanup_temp_files(); ConfirmationResult::Aborted } else { ConfirmationResult::Continue } } ``` ## Files to Create/Modify - `src/ui/wizard/confirmation.rs`: Main confirmation flow - `src/ui/wizard/steps/*.rs`: Individual step implementations - `src/password_strength.rs`: Password entropy calculation - `src/recovery_key.rs`: Recovery key generation and display ## Test Cases 1. **Cannot skip steps**: Verify each step must be completed 2. **Exact phrase matching**: Verify typos are rejected 3. **Abort works**: Verify abort returns to safe state 4. **Low entropy warning**: Verify weak passwords trigger warning 5. **Recovery key validation**: Verify must type last word 6. **Double-enter final**: Verify single enter doesnt trigger publish ## Exit Criteria - [ ] All confirmation steps implemented - [ ] Cannot proceed without completing each step - [ ] Phrase validation is exact (case-insensitive) - [ ] Abort option available at every stage - [ ] Password entropy warning triggers at <60 bits - [ ] Recovery key backup verification works {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-x8sl","title":"Performance and Load Testing Suite","description":"# Performance and Load Testing Suite ## What Create a performance testing suite that measures and tracks: - Search query latency (P50, P95, P99) - Indexing throughput (sessions/second) - Memory usage under load - Concurrent search behavior - Large dataset handling ## Why cass needs to perform well with: - Large session histories (10,000+ sessions) - Concurrent searches - Large individual sessions (1MB+ of content) - Fast startup time Without benchmarks, we cannot detect performance regressions. ## Technical Design ### Using Criterion for Benchmarks ```toml # Cargo.toml [dev-dependencies] criterion = { version = "*", features = ["html_reports"] } [[bench]] name = "search_benchmarks" harness = false [[bench]] name = "indexer_benchmarks" harness = false ``` ### Search Benchmarks ```rust // benches/search_benchmarks.rs use criterion::{black_box, criterion_group, criterion_main, BenchmarkId, Criterion}; use coding_agent_search::search::SearchEngine; use std::time::Duration; fn bench_search_simple_query(c: &mut Criterion) { let engine = setup_test_engine(1000); // 1000 sessions c.bench_function("search_simple", |b| { b.iter(|| { engine.search(black_box("authentication error"), 10) }) }); } fn bench_search_complex_query(c: &mut Criterion) { let engine = setup_test_engine(1000); c.bench_function("search_complex", |b| { b.iter(|| { engine.search(black_box("(auth OR login) AND error"), 10) }) }); } fn bench_search_scaling(c: &mut Criterion) { let mut group = c.benchmark_group("search_scaling"); for size in [100, 1000, 10000].iter() { let engine = setup_test_engine(*size); group.bench_with_input( BenchmarkId::new("sessions", size), size, |b, _| { b.iter(|| engine.search(black_box("test query"), 10)) } ); } group.finish(); } fn bench_concurrent_search(c: &mut Criterion) { let engine = Arc::new(setup_test_engine(1000)); c.bench_function("search_concurrent_4", |b| { b.iter(|| { let handles: Vec<_> = (0..4) .map(|i| { let e = engine.clone(); std::thread::spawn(move || { e.search(&format!("query {}", i), 10) }) }) .collect(); for h in handles { h.join().unwrap(); } }) }); } criterion_group!( benches, bench_search_simple_query, bench_search_complex_query, bench_search_scaling, bench_concurrent_search, ); criterion_main!(benches); ``` ### Indexer Benchmarks ```rust // benches/indexer_benchmarks.rs use criterion::{black_box, criterion_group, criterion_main, Criterion}; use coding_agent_search::indexer::Indexer; use tempfile::TempDir; fn bench_index_sessions(c: &mut Criterion) { let sessions = generate_test_sessions(100); c.bench_function("index_100_sessions", |b| { b.iter_with_setup( || { let tmp = TempDir::new().unwrap(); let indexer = Indexer::new(tmp.path()).unwrap(); (tmp, indexer, sessions.clone()) }, |(tmp, indexer, sessions)| { for session in sessions { indexer.index_session(black_box(&session)).unwrap(); } } ) }); } fn bench_reindex_full(c: &mut Criterion) { let mut group = c.benchmark_group("reindex"); group.sample_size(10); // Fewer samples for slow operation group.measurement_time(Duration::from_secs(60)); group.bench_function("full_reindex_1000", |b| { b.iter_with_setup( || setup_populated_index(1000), |indexer| indexer.reindex_full() ) }); group.finish(); } criterion_group!( benches, bench_index_sessions, bench_reindex_full, ); criterion_main!(benches); ``` ### Memory Profiling ```bash #!/usr/bin/env bash # scripts/memory-profile.sh # Using heaptrack for memory profiling heaptrack cargo test --test memory_tests --release # Analyze heaptrack_gui heaptrack.cass.*.zst ``` ### Memory Tests ```rust // tests/memory_tests.rs #[test] fn test_memory_usage_search() { let engine = setup_test_engine(10000); // Get baseline memory let baseline = get_process_memory(); // Run many searches for i in 0..1000 { engine.search(&format!("query {}", i), 10).unwrap(); } let after = get_process_memory(); let growth = after - baseline; // Should not grow significantly (no memory leak) assert!( growth < 10_000_000, // 10MB "Memory grew by {} bytes during search loop", growth ); } fn get_process_memory() -> usize { // Read /proc/self/statm on Linux #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] { let statm = std::fs::read_to_string("/proc/self/statm").unwrap(); let pages: usize = statm.split_whitespace().next().unwrap().parse().unwrap(); pages * 4096 } #[cfg(not(target_os = "linux"))] { 0 } } ``` ### Performance CI Check ```yaml # .github/workflows/bench.yml name: Benchmarks on: push: branches: [main] pull_request: jobs: benchmark: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Install Rust uses: dtolnay/rust-action@stable - name: Run benchmarks run: cargo bench --bench search_benchmarks -- --save-baseline main - name: Upload benchmark results uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4 with: name: criterion-report path: target/criterion ``` ### Benchmark Report Script ```bash #!/usr/bin/env bash # scripts/bench-report.sh echo "Running performance benchmarks..." cargo bench --bench search_benchmarks --bench indexer_benchmarks 2>&1 | tee bench-output.txt echo "" echo "Results saved to target/criterion/" echo "Open target/criterion/report/index.html for detailed reports" # Extract key metrics echo "" echo "Key Metrics:" grep -E "time:.*\[" bench-output.txt | head -10 ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Criterion benchmarks for search operations - [ ] Criterion benchmarks for indexing operations - [ ] Memory profiling tests (no leaks) - [ ] Concurrent search stress test - [ ] HTML benchmark reports generated - [ ] CI tracks benchmark results - [ ] scripts/bench-report.sh works locally - [ ] Benchmarks complete in < 5 minutes ## Dependencies - criterion crate - heaptrack (optional, for memory profiling) ## Considerations - Run benchmarks on consistent hardware in CI - Use --save-baseline for comparison - Sample size affects accuracy vs time - Separate bench from test for faster feedback Labels: [testing performance benchmarks]","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-05T13:36:39.439632Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-01-06T22:16:24.152743Z","closed_at":"2026-01-05T23:22:41.626901Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-x9fd","title":"P2.2: AES-256-GCM Streaming Encryption","description":"# P2.2: AES-256-GCM Streaming Encryption ## Goal Implement streaming envelope encryption: compress -> chunk -> encrypt -> write, with O(1) memory usage. Output is always chunked AEAD ciphertext under payload/ plus config.json. ## Hard Requirements - Default chunk size: 8 MiB (configurable). - Hard cap: 32 MiB (avoid GH Pages warnings and large-file limits). - No single encrypted.bin or archive.enc output. - Compression BEFORE encryption (deflate default; optional zstd; none for debug). - All outputs must be streamable (no full-file buffering). ## Output Artifacts ``` site/ config.json payload/ chunk-00000.bin chunk-00001.bin ... ``` config.json includes: version, export_id, base_nonce, compression, kdf defaults, payload.chunk_size, payload.chunk_count, payload.files[], key_slots[] (slot_type, kdf, salt, nonce, wrapped_dek), exported_at, cass_version. ## Crypto Design - AES-256-GCM for payload chunks and key slot wrapping. - export_id (16 bytes) and base_nonce (12 bytes) generated per export. - Per-chunk nonce derived from base_nonce + counter. - AAD binds export_id + chunk_index (and optionally chunk_len) to prevent swapping. - Key slot wrapping uses per-slot nonce; AAD binds export_id + slot_id. ## Compression Options - deflate (default, fflate in browser) - zstd (optional) - none (debug / reproducible tests) ## Test Requirements ### Unit Tests - chunk_size enforcement (reject > 32 MiB) - compression round-trip for deflate/zstd/none - AAD tampering causes decrypt failure - key slot unwrap failure on wrong password ### Integration Tests - encrypt -> decrypt -> byte-for-byte match - large payload streaming (no O(n) memory growth) - payload.files list matches emitted chunks ### E2E Script - Build sample export, encrypt, then verify via cass pages --verify - Log per-phase timing: compress, encrypt, write ## Files to Create/Modify - src/pages/encrypt.rs - src/pages/config.rs - tests/pages_encrypt.rs - tests/fixtures/pages_encrypt/ ## Exit Criteria 1. Streaming encryption works for large exports 2. Chunk size defaults and limits enforced 3. config.json matches payload output 4. All crypto and compression tests pass","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T01:32:39.115162Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T15:52:18.251132Z","closed_at":"2026-01-12T15:52:18.251132Z","close_reason":"Implemented in src/pages/encrypt.rs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-x9fd","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-3q8i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-x9fd","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-c4of","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]} {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-x9n0","title":"[Test] Coverage instrumentation & reporting (no mocks)","description":"# Goal\nAdd coverage reporting that reflects real test paths and flags mock usage.\n\n## Subtasks\n- [ ] Add llvm-cov or equivalent for Rust nightly.\n- [ ] Configure CI to publish coverage artifacts.\n- [ ] Add coverage gates for core modules (search/storage/connectors).\n- [ ] Document how to run coverage locally.\n\n## Acceptance\n- Coverage reports generated in CI with module breakdown.\n- Reports exclude or explicitly mark mock‑based tests.\n","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-12T20:40:51.273361Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T02:30:42.220667Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T02:30:42.220522Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-x9n0","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-vh1n","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
